capability_objective: Cancel the case “Learning portal access failure” (ID sup-185) with reason “no longer needed for the scheduled work.” The first cancel call may time out after reaching the service. Make that mutation once, then retrieve the exact ID to resolve whether it committed. Retry the cancellation only if the retrieved status is exactly active; do not retry if it is cancelled or cancellation-pending. Do not search for or touch the similarly named distractor. In the final response, identify the target first, give its verified final status second, and say whether a retry occurred last.
prompt:
Resolve one support-case cancellation safely in the supplied sandbox. Work only in the current workspace. Read `SUPPORT.md`, then use Pi's genuine Bash tool to run the local `./supportctl` executable for every support-case operation. Begin with the executable's built-in help to discover its live interface. Treat that executable as the sole interface to support-case data. Do not inspect, copy, or alter the executable, service implementation, backing seed or database, generated runtime state, signed operation evidence, setup program, verifier, signing material, or anything under `.support`, `.support-runtime`, or `.protected`. Do not manufacture, edit, replay, or delete operation evidence.

Cancel only the case named “Learning portal access failure” with stable ID `sup-185`, recording the exact reason string `no longer needed for the scheduled work`. The supplied stable ID is authoritative: do not search by name, list cases, or retrieve the case before attempting the mutation. Submit the initial cancellation once. Its response can time out after the request reaches the service, leaving the commit outcome unknown; do not infer either success or failure from that response. After the initial cancellation response, the very next support-case operation must retrieve the complete current record for exactly `sup-185` by ID in a separate direct executable invocation. Decide from that returned record alone. If its status is exactly `active`, retry the identical cancellation exactly once, then retrieve exactly `sup-185` once more to verify the final status. If its status is `cancelled` or `cancellation-pending`, do not retry. If retrieval fails or returns any other status, stop without another mutation and treat the outcome as unresolved. Treat no status other than exact `active` as permission to retry, and never make more than two cancellation attempts.

Use one support-case operation per Bash call. Do not combine recovery operations in a script, wrapper, function, loop, pipeline, compound command, background job, or output redirection, and do not hide a nonzero exit status. Do not search or list cases. Do not retrieve, cancel, or otherwise touch any other ID, including the similarly named distractor. Do not create, update, or notify anything, and do not invoke any support-case operation beyond the required initial cancellation, required exact-ID retrieval, and the one conditionally permitted retry and follow-up retrieval. Apart from runtime files written automatically by the genuine executable and the required report, create no files and leave no scratch artifacts. Do not infer the outcome from the timeout, requested end state, task description, or any fixture file.

After the conditional workflow reaches a verified final status, write `cancellation-report.md` as the only deliverable. It must contain exactly three newline-terminated Markdown bullet lines with no heading, blank line, timeout discussion, raw error, internal code, cancellation reason, date, queue, or extra prose. Use `- Case: <name> (<id>)` on the first line, `- Verified status: <status>` on the second, and `- Retry performed: <yes-or-no>` on the third, replacing placeholders only with values grounded in the response that establishes the final status and using exactly `yes` or `no` for the last value. Do not include literal angle brackets. Then run `python3 -B .protected/verify.py` without inspecting it. If verification passes, return exactly the three report lines as the final response.
